speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] interview. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to backtrack for a second. This Death March-- why it was called a Death March because many thousands that could not walk anymore and we just had to drop them on the side of the road. And as the SS guards who were guarding us on each side, you know, before they left anybody behind, they shot them in the head. So they were not-- they were not leaving anybody alive behind. And many thousands were lost just through these death marches.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we'll go forward to Mauthausen. As I'm going up to the camp, passing the quarries, and these were prisoners from Mauthausen-- Jewish prisoners, mainly-- that were cutting these rocks in the middle of the winter in this quarry. And I could see these steps-- I don't know, about a hundred steps-- going up to the top. But they were working on the side of this face of this cliff. And it looked like a very bad place. I was lucky that I didn't have to stay in Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into the camp, and there were thousands of us crammed in from all these different transports because, at this time, there were transports coming from all over in the east into Austrian camps. So we were taken to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] showers. We had to get undressed, so the few pieces of good clothes that I had, I lost it all. And we went through the showers and came out again on the other side just with my boots on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, in fact, I was there for four days. And for three days, they didn't know what to do with us. We were sitting in these barrack rooms, naked, you know? Like, one had to sit down first and the other one sat in your-- close to your body. You had to spread your legs. And this is the way we were packed in there because for the lack of space in Mauthausen. And this was a very uncomfortable way to sit for two days like that. You know? You couldn't get up and it was just terrible, you know? And you couldn't go to the latrines or anything like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we were allowed to come out of this barrack, and we were given our striped outfits. And I was taken to a camp called Melk, which was also further west of Mauthausen on the Danube River. And we were in this old barrack that was used by cavalry in the Austro-Hungarian-- in the first World War, I think. And it was on top of a hill. On one side, we could see this huge monastery, which is very famous-- the Melk monastery. And below us was the railroad station and the Danube River. And I was put into a detail. There were many Russian prisoners of war, as well as prisoners here, who did not get any other treatment. They received the same treatment as we did, even though they were prisoners of war. I was working on a detail where we were taken, every day, on a train, well, in boxcars, with two SS men inside, in the middle of the boxcar, through a tunnel. And we worked in a big place that had-- they were digging six shafts into the mountain. And these shafts were of such size that I saw a locomotive with a whole bunch of boxcars drive right into the mountain where there were-- I think, aircraft parts were being built.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was on a detail where we were drilling another shaft. And I was given an air hammer, which is quite heavy. And, you know, for my age of 15, you know, I could hardly lift this darn thing. And we had to drill in this rock, like, steps going up to the top and drill it in a-- in a oval fashion, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't really handle this hammer too well, this air hammer. And this work detail was run by the Todt organization, which was a civilian German organization that was building all these industrial sites for the German government. They used to wear a black [INAUDIBLE] uniform. They did not wear any guns or whatsoever. And I think this man had some mercy on me, and he took me off from this air hammer. And he told me to take all the bits from these air drills, which used to break when you're drilling into rock-- hard rock. And I used to collect all these bits and I made, from a wire, sort of like a holder and I used to drop these bits in. And I had a handle on this wire cage kind of thing. And I used to take about six or eight bits at a time to the smithy. I used to jump on these long conveyors that's used to take you out of the shaft and the smithy was outside in the yard. And it was warm there, because, you know, there was heat. There was coal burning where they were heating up these bits, and they were sharpening it and putting them together, so I was able to catch a-- a bit of a sleep there. And I was there for about approximately no more than four weeks. I remember one incident. They were always-- you know, there were many conveyor belts, from side shafts coming into a main shaft. There was a big conveyor that was taking the stuff out to a main silo.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, one of these people from the Todt organization said that the conveyor belt was sabotaged, and somebody cut out a piece from the conveyor belt for a sole of a shoe, you know? And that was a very serious problem. So as our detail, on the way-- when we were-- when our shift was finished-- I was working on the night shift-- we were all lined up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS officer came, and he said, who cut, who sabotaged the conveyor belt. And, of course, there were hundreds of us. I don't know how many. Nobody stepped forward. And he says, you have two minutes, whoever did this, to come forward. And after two minutes, nobody came forward.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he just went and he counted-- he told his orderly to count every 10th man in the line and take out-- bring out the 10th man. And so they collected several people. I think there were about 12 or so all together. And they were lined up against the wall and shot on the spot. And I was lucky to be right in the middle, in between the 10, you know? And so these are the kind of things that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember towards the end of my time there in this-- in Melk, in these shafts, the last day somebody-- the grapevine came down inside the shaft that they are mining the entrances to the shafts, that they were drilling big holes, you know, with drills, and they were packing in a lot of explosives. And we were inside these shafts, so we thought this is-- this is what they're going to do to us. But fortunately, that didn't happen. When we were coming out of the shafts, we could see a boxcar at every shaft opening. And there were people loading a lot of explosive into the holes that were dug on each side of the shaft. We were taken back to Melk. And this was the-- by this time, Vienna was being bombed by the Americans on a daily basis. And I guess the Russian troops were not far from Vienna, coming from the east. And the people from Vienna were all running away from the Russians. I remember this very vividly, because they were deathly scared to be occupied by the Russians, you know, when the Russians will come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were running and the Danube was full. The river barges were-- people were-- full of people on the river barges. I remember seeing the train at the railroad station. People were hanging from the roof. You know, these were Austrians running away from the Russians on the-- on the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were going with carts and they were pulling little wagons and everybody was moving to-- towards the Americans. They wanted to be-- they wanted to get to the Americans, you know, and running away from the Russians. We were taken out of Melk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been in Melk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Melk [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] February-- I arrived in Mauthausen probably around the 5th of-- 5th-- between the 5th and 10th of February, and I was there for four days, so let's say the 15th. And I was there till end of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living in these barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The barracks here were not bad, because this was an old barrack, you know, from the cavalry barrack. It wasn't that bad. We had bunk beds again. You know, the food was very scarce. It was the same old diet, you know, that we had everywhere else. You know, that didn't change with the location. So the food was quite scarce.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had-- all of my-- the people that I was sleeping with they were all Russian. These were all Russian prisoners. And they were really nice to me, you know? They were all mature men, and they were all military men. And they really knew how to survive a lot better than I did, but I did survive til then, too, you know? And I remember picking up a few Russian words, you know, because Russian was totally different to me. And they were very good. You know? What kind of things did they help you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't give me any food, but they didn't bother me either and they didn't take anything away from me. You were sort of on your own. There was nothing, no help that you could get from anybody. If somebody who was stronger tolerated you, didn't lean on you, or didn't bother you, that was-- that was a big thing, you know? So that was great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working with other Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't know. It was a mixed kind of group, because a lot of us came from Auschwitz, and these were mostly Jews. And the people that were in Melk ahead of me, these were mostly Russians. So it was a mixed group of people. You know, there were Jews from all over-- people from all over the world. There were Greeks-- we called them Grecos. There were French, people from Holland, you know, from Yugoslavia, and all over the place. Italianos-- we called the Italians Italianos. I know that the Greeks, they really had a tough time. They could not-- they really suffered a lot under that climate, because they came from a much warmer climate. And they did not do too well in Auschwitz. They-- they went down to nothing very fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- we called them musalims, for some reason, you know? That's the name they gave these people who-- who were just ready to drop any time. The-- the look in their eyes sort of changed, and we called them musalim, like after a Muslim, I think. And soon after, they were gone, you know? They died. So Melk was liquidated, and we were stuffed in these river barges that was pulled on the Danube. One motor-- one boat, sort of like a tugboat, was pulling about six of these barges on cables. And we were stuffed inside the hold of these barges, and we were pulled up on the Danube to a town called Linz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we disembarked in Linz, and I remember, going through Linz, there was a lot of bomb damage, you know. Craters all over the place, you know, like from bombs. And we were marching up-- this was early April and the weather was really nice. We marched for three days. I remember the name of the towns-- [INAUDIBLE] Lambach, Gmunden, Wells. I remember these three stops we made. We slept in fields, you know. And I remember there was-- we slept in a field near the little forest and I was digging in the ground trying to find some-- I did find a couple of potatoes from the previous year. I was lucky to. But even grass was-- if we found some grass we were eating grass, because that was better than nothing at all. And we were marching on the road. And I remember one day-- this was very unfortunate that it happened-- behind this forest, all of a sudden, we hear planes flying. And I could see sparks, like, you know-- I know these were tracer bullets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these were American fighter planes, and they came and they shot us right up in the middle of the road, because they-- they were just shooting at everything that was moving, you know? And they didn't-- couldn't distinguish who we were from troops, you know, I suppose. So many people were just killed. They went right through the column, you know. And when we heard these planes, you know, before we had a chance to run to the ditch, you know, there were many, many killed, you know, by bullets. And we just kept on marching. We left those people behind. So I got into this camp called Ebensee. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was early April, or perhaps the first week in April. And the camp was chock full of people. I don't know. Perhaps 20,000 people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Conditions were awful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for about a week in a cement place. But soon after that-- a week or 10 days-- the work was finished and we were back in camp. And, in fact, the camp was closed down, and the water was shut off. They did not pipe any water into the camp. And, of course, food they didn't give us. And the dead bodies were not-- you know, they were just piling up on the square. And in the back of the barracks, in the latrines, you know, anybody that died in their bunk, you know, if somebody else wanted to take over the bunk, they were just dumped in the back of the barrack in the latrines.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were people like cord-wood, you know. Grotesque sights, you know? And in the square, there were mountains, you know, piling up and-- of dead bodies. And there was nobody doing anything. And there was typhus. You know, we were infested with lice, and we were so filthy. And I had typhus like everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess a couple weeks after all this-- this terrible ordeal, we are now at May the 6th, and I remember I was on this bunk and somebody that could still walk, he went out and he came back, and he says that there is a white flag flying on the tower near the gate on the guard tower. So-- and I could hear him say this, but I thought I was in a feverish dream or something. And somebody else went out and checked this and he said, yes, that's-- there is a white flag and all the German guards have left for the-- from the towers. So I thought that this was something really serious, and I crawled out from my bunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I sort of crawled on my stomach into the square, you know? And it was-- I was facing the gates to the camp. And I could hear some explosions and some heavy equipment moving, and I didn't know what it was because I couldn't see it. But, all of a sudden, the camp gate came down, and there was a tank inside the camp. And I could see a white star on this tank, and I thought this must be an American tank. And there were soldiers sitting on the turret of this tank with their guns. And I'll never forget-- this was-- this will always stay with me. I remember the eyes of these soldiers, they were like saucers, you know? Because what they saw, you know, the-- you know, as they came through, it must have been a terrible sight, you know? These thousands of dead bodies piled up and the smell, the stink, and-- They saw, those that could walk, they looked like ghosts. They were running towards the tank and they tried to touch the soldiers. And they were scared, the soldiers. They didn't want to be touched by these people. I remember that it was so comical. They tried to climb higher and higher on this turret, you know, the soldiers. There was nowhere to climb anymore, but they were just looking down and they were absolutely frightened, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the tanks stopped, because it couldn't go. There were bodies all over the place. And I remember Jeeps were coming up and these officers were-- or soldiers were getting out of their jeeps, and there was a-- I remember there was somebody, he says, I am a Rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was really something, you know? I said, there's a Rabbi in a uniform. And he said, everything is fine. You know, you're liberated. And, you know, we'll take care of you. And there were other priests. There were-- there were reverends, you know, chaplains from the army. And they wanted to know who's a Catholic, who is a Protestant. And, you know, they-- for some of them, they only got there in time to give them their last rites, actually, because, you know, our systems were so far gone, you know? It was like a clock winding down, you know? And I was laying there and what I was thinking, I says, I'm-- I'm happy-- I'm liberated. I'm not sure that I could say that I was happy. I felt that I'm safe more than happy, you know? I knew that there's no Nazi can hurt me anymore. This was the feeling that I had. But I had this terrible sight in front of me. You know, that did not make me feel happy. And I was thinking, did anybody else survive from my family? This was the first thing that I was thinking about. And so I was just laying there, you know, saying, you know, that's it. I don't have to worry. Somebody will take care of me. This was the most important thing, that I didn't have to look out, you know, how to survive another minute, you see? And that was important. So, you know, there was a lot of movement of big trucks coming up. I saw field kitchens being pulled up by the Army. And I remember this huge trailer coming up. I remember sides of beef hanging down, you know? I thought this was really something. I never saw so much food in a long time. [LAUGHS] I mean, for a whole year, I didn't see any like that. What was the food like before the Americans arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, before the Americans came in Ebensee, for two and 1/2-- two to two and 1/2 weeks, there was absolutely no food. We received absolutely nothing. They shut the camp down. They shut the water off.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I was going out to look for water, you know, and there was-- there was a-- a big cistern there for fire purposes, you know? They were collecting water in it, you know? In case there is a fire, they can use the water from this big pool, you know? And we were trying to get water, and there were dead bodies in this pool, you know? And we were trying to get the [NON-ENGLISH] down with a string, you know, to somehow get water out of it. That's the only thing we had. There was absolutely nothing to drink. There was no food. So at this stage, I wasn't even hungry anymore. You know, I-- we had no stomachs left. I was just hanging on, you know? Barely hanging on. But hunger was no more-- you know, it wasn't the-- the big thing, you know, I suppose. But when I saw all this food-- and a little while later, I could smell this food. The Americans were cooking this food, you know? And people were going crazy. You know, those that could walk they were running towards the smell where it was coming from. And I was just laying there-- laying there-- laying on the ground. I did not want to run to this kitchen and be trampled on by a mob, you know? Because I had experiences, you know, when we had a chance sometimes to go to a shower and we had a door opening and a thousand people tried to go through at the same time. And you sort of get lifted up, you know, and if you're dropped, you're trampled to death. And I said, I don't ever want to be in a line ever again. So-- and I couldn't go. I didn't have the strength to go there. And it was very fortunate, because many of those that did get there, they got a big pot full of stew, you know? And they ate it. They wolfed it down, you know, and many thousands, they just dropped like flies. They-- they died right on the spot, you know? Because they could not take this food in their belly, you know? We were not used to food for a whole year. And our system was completely shot. And so somebody gave me one bite of the stew, and I had this one bite and I just about brought up my whole insides, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the Americans realized that, you know, we cannot handle this food. They didn't know how we're-- how they were going to look after these people. They just came in. They thought, give them food. And it was ironic, you know, that, you know, we died without-- for lack of food. And now that they gave us food, you know, that killed us, too. That was a tragedy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they cut this food out, and they gave us crackers and some powdered milk. And the medical-- the medical units came up, you know, from the army, you know, the-- and they had to start to look after us. You know, we were there-- you know, I cannot tell you the confusion with thousands of dead bodies all over and us laying there, you know, filthy, stinking, mess, you know, of people. The living people they had to-- they wore masks. They cut these rags off from us and they put up showers. They put up a hospital tent with cots. And they cut these rags off, and they tried to wash our bodies. You know, we had like a snake skin, you know? It was the caked in dirt from months and months that we couldn't wash. It took weeks to get that off. And and they-- they put on DDT on us, you know? They tried to kill the lice, you know, and all that sort of thing. And finally we were put on a cot in this tent, and that really felt good. You know, I had my own cot, I didn't have to roll on a piece of board.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because after, eventually, you know, we couldn't-- it was the hardest thing to lay on these boards, because our bones were sticking out, and you know, they were sore. You know, you were just aching. Because, you know, you had no padding anywhere. You know, you were strictly laying on bones. So that was a great feeling to lay on a cot, you know, these folding cots they had in this tent.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they looked after us, and about a month later, they encouraged us to leave the camp. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember many guys that were healthier than I was, they went down into the town of Ebensee. And they went in the German homes and they took out whatever they wanted-- clothing, and food, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a week-- about a week after I was liberated, I-- I said, I'm going to go down, too. And I got to know somebody, another boy, and we went together. I was afraid to go myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was even afraid to go out of the camp. I was-- I had such a feeling of not being wanted. I didn't know what kind of world this is that did this to me. And even though it was a camp, but I was kind of safe here now. You know? And to go out, it was-- again, you know, trying a new world, you know? So I remember I went down with this boy, and we went into a German home-- an Austrian home. This was in Austria. And I was absolutely amazed at what I saw. They had a [NON-ENGLISH]. That was a room for food. And it was loaded with food, you know? With preserves, you know, bottles and bottles. And clothing-- I remember seeing a picture of it. She said, this is my husband in an SS uniform. And I remember she saying, we didn't know. We didn't know what was happening up in that camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I always said that if I'll ever survive, I'll kill every German, you know? But I-- I just couldn't wait to get out of that house, you know? I didn't-- I didn't feel right to do this somehow. So she gave us some food. And I didn't have any clothing. I was in rags, actually. And in fact, when I had to leave the camp to come home, what I came home to Slovakia in, I found a Hitler Youth shirt, which everybody could tell from a mile away the color of it and all that, and a pair of britches that belonged to Hitler Youth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to cut it down, because my body was filling up with a lot of water. And my legs were this big. I couldn't put these pants on. And I had a pair of boots that I couldn't put my feet into these boots anymore. I had to cut the back of the boots down, and I just put my toes in like a pair of slippers. And I tied the heels to my ankle with some string. And this is the way I went back to Czechoslovakia. One day, the Americans put us on a truck, and they delivered us to a town called Ceske Budejovice. And this was on a Sunday afternoon. It was a beautiful day, and, you know, the Czechs are-- they were all sitting in the beer gardens there on the sidewalk, and drinking and singing away. And when they saw this truck, and they saw our-- we got off, they invited us into the restaurants, and they were plying us with drink and food. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, it was-- they wanted to do all the best things for us. But this was the worst thing for us, really, because we couldn't refuse any food or drink. And I had diarrhea, and I remember the three weeks that it took me to get back to my town in Slovakia, wherever we stopped, people were helping us with food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sick all the way. I had diarrhea. I had such terrible problems, you know? I couldn't keep food down and-- and yet, I couldn't refuse food, you know? It was a real dilemma. And so I arrived in this town of [INAUDIBLE]. And now this-- we were Slovakia again, you know? This is the way things change, you see? Like after the war, where I was in Hungary, our town became Czechoslovakia again, because the Hungarians lost the war and so Czechoslovakia became a country again. We took back-- they took back all the lost areas. So I came to [INAUDIBLE], and I went to the place where there's a market and the farmers come in from all the neighboring towns. And I thought that maybe I can find a farmer from my town and hitch a ride back home. And so I went there, and I was looking for a face that I would recognize, and I recognized a farmer from my town. And I told him who I was, and he didn't recognize me. He didn't know who I was. You know? I just didn't look like this person, you know, like I did a year ago. And I asked him whether he can give me a lift back home, and he said, OK.
